module_mood_contract: expanded horizon — 'oh, we just did the thing we're worried about doing'. Forward awe with a deliverable in hand. Edits that make this feel like a tidy graduation ceremony steal the forward hunger.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood contract named and honoured per beat.
Mood doesn't resolve early — no tidy completion before the close.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Line 30 ('When the file saves, you're done with Phase 1') and line 135 ('You are done with Phase 2...') are operational completion markers, not graduation-ceremony beats. Phase 3 ends open-ended: 'Whenever the group says stop, stop.' No resolution of the M2 expanded-horizon mood before it's due.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Teaching moment lands reliably across persona-skill variation.
rules_evaluated.evidence: The magic beat — room ran a diverge-synthesize-cross-pollinate system without being told it was architecture — is felt through lived experience in all three phases. The reveal is in maintainer block (line 189) for the organisers to name verbally after Phase 3. It does not depend on the participant reading the maintainer block. The forcing function is the exercise mechanics, not prose narration. Trace phase 2 mood_score=9, phase 3 mood_score=9 confirm the beat lands before naming.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Big Idea fidelity — file honours the module's Big Idea from strategy doc.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Strategy doc M2 Big Idea: 'The hard part isn't picking a tool — it's finding the crux. Good synthesis comes before good cross-pollination.' The entire exercise is structured around finding the crux with group push-back, and synthesis is gated before cross-pollination begins (line 135: 'You are done with Phase 2 when your group synthesis is saved...'). The crux-as-Rumelt concept is earned at line 50 before the synthesis prompt.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Body teaches the work; trainer manages the room outside text.
rules_evaluated.evidence: No authored conversation cues, pause beats, comparison prompts, or 'discuss with...' sentences found in student-facing body (lines 1-180). The 'pause' at line 174 is inside a fenced prompt block instructing Claude, not a body-prose cohort-management directive. Reveal is in maintainer block only (line 189).

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Forcing functions live in prompts, not body prose.
rules_evaluated.evidence: All key forcing mechanics are inside fenced prompt blocks: one-question-at-a-time (lines 22-27), push-back-once (line 23), propose-crux-in-chat-first / do-not-save-yet (lines 64-79), cycle-and-narrate mechanics (lines 150-176). Body prose around prompts directs the student to paste or steer but does not embed the forcing functions themselves. The 'push back until the crux sounds like the group's real trouble' (line 99) is a steering instruction, not a forcing function — fits the optional-iteration carve-out (rule 16 carve-out: optional push-back prompts can stay loose in body).

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Meta-frame lectures are closers, not openers.
rules_evaluated.evidence: No lecture block in this exercise. The crux concept is earned inline (line 50, FYI) before the synthesis prompt — this is a one-breath earn, not a lecture.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Per-exercise leap test — three observable Monday-morning outcomes in maintainer block.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Maintainer block (lines 181-199) contains Meta, Quality, Strategy reference, and Eval instance entries. No leap test section naming three observable outcomes the rollout custodian exhibits by the next working day. Story judge blocks ship on missing leap tests per rule 45.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add a Leap test entry to the maintainer block naming three observable post-workshop behaviors, e.g.: (1) participant can articulate their org's crux in one sentence when asked by a colleague, (2) driver can re-run the group synthesis pattern in a new context using Cowork + shared folder, (3) participant's first-name rollout-notes.md exists and is legible to others.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Per-phase failure mode and escape hatch — every forcing function phase documents dominant failure + one recovery move.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 1 forcing function (one-question-at-a-time interview with push-back) has no documented failure mode or escape hatch in the maintainer block. Phase 2 has a partial failure-mode callout in body at line 95 ('In the dry run, Claude dismissed...') but no formal maintainer-block row with escape hatch. Phase 3 has no failure mode or escape hatch documented. Rule 47 requires the maintainer block to name both for each phase shipping a forcing function.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add a Failure modes block to the maintainer section for each phase: Phase 1 dominant failure = participant gives vague one-line answers, Claude's push-back doesn't surface the real concern; escape = trainer models a sharper answer live ('watch me run mine first'). Phase 2 dominant failure = group accepts Claude's first crux proposal without push-back; escape = organiser prompts 'what would your skeptic say about this crux?'. Phase 3 dominant failure = driver sends prompt and sits back, treating it as spectator sport rather than steering the cycle; escape = trainer narrates one correction live before letting the room run.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Hard-grep phrase list: no room-share commands in student-facing body.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Grep of lines 1-180 finds no instances of 'tell the person', 'sitting next to you', 'share with your neighbor', 'tell the room', 'say X out loud', 'name X in chat', 'call it out', 'read aloud', 'say it aloud', 'sit with', 'take a moment', 'let it land', 'pause and reflect', 'breathe'.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood-floor 8+ on every teaching beat; setup beats are exempt.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Trace phase scores: Phase 1 = 8, Phase 2 = 9, Phase 3 = 9, close = 9. All teaching beats at or above floor. No beat below 7.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Heavy fan-out prompts get a body-side recovery callout.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 3 cross-pollination prompt (lines 149-177) is a continuous-cycle fan-out: Claude runs indefinitely, visits multiple files per cycle, re-reads, narrates, refreshes recommendations. Body at line 141-143 offers light framing ('One useful comment is better than rushing through every file') but does not explicitly name (a) heavy run expected, (b) recovery move when narration runs long or drifts. The exercise body provides steer examples ('narrower, only flag overlaps with our crux', 'slow down, one cycle then wait for me', or just 'stop') at lines 143-144, which partially satisfies the callout requirement. However, the callout does not front-name that this is a heavy/long-running prompt — the steer signals are framed as interruption options, not as a pre-prompt recovery contract. Rule 48 requires the callout BEFORE the prompt.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add a short callout before the Phase 3 fenced prompt naming: 'This prompt runs in cycles until you say stop — expect it to run for several minutes. If narration runs long or Claude visits more files than useful, interrupt: narrow the scope or say stop.' The existing steering examples (lines 143-144) can stay as they are.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Module-to-module arc — close hands off to next module's open.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Strategy doc M2 bridge: 'The live session named the rollout crux. The homework builds the personal system and verification habit.' Exercise does not close with a tidy graduation but with an open system surface (the synthesis file + comments + chat recommendations) and explicitly notes 'anyone can rebuild them later by reading the file' (line 179). This hands off cleanly to M3's 'capable next step' mood. No M3 teaching moment is front-run.
